item.title: Ocular Dominance Columns: Example
item.description: Figure 1. Ocular dominance columns (see red and blue stripes) in the primary visual cortex (V1). The V1/V2 border is shown as white ribbon above the surface. A more detailed description of the surface and how it relates to an actual human brain can be found in the text body. Note that there is a pause in the middle of the video for a better view on V1.

item.title: Ocular Dominance Columns: Collection of Articles
item.description: This blog post is aimed to give a list of all ocular dominance columns studies done in the field of human fMRI so far. Only studies which tried to map the columnar pattern are mentioned. Neither decoding nor pure analysis papers are listed here. If someone reads this post and misses one or more articles, I would be very pleased to be notified. The list might be extended in the future.

item.title: GBB Method: Toy Example
item.description: Introduction High-resolution functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) at ultra-high magnetic field strengths of 7 Tesla and above extends the possibilities to study brain functioning in the living human brain. One of the most exciting purposes of high-resolution fMRI is the differentiation of brain activation within the cerebral cortex. This information is especially valuable since it is known that most of the cortex is functionally organized into so called cortical layers and columns.

item.title: About
item.description: I am a PhD student in the Neurophysics department at the Max Planck Institute for Human Cognitive and Brain Sciences (MPI-CBS) in Leipzig, Germany. I studied physics at the Free University in Berlin and at the Leipzig University, where I extended my studies to musicology and philosophy. During my Master studies in physics, I joined the Neurophysics department at the MPI-CBS to write my Master Thesis in the field of high-resolution fMRI at ultra-high magnetic field strengths.

item.title: Contact
item.description: Mail: daniel.haenelt@gmail.com (personal) / haenelt@cbs.mpg.de (work) Phone: +49 (0) 341 9940 2438 Address: Max Planck Institute for Human Cognitive and Brain Sciences, Stephanstraße 1a, 04103 Leipzig, Germany

item.title: Publications
item.description: Articles Attar, F. M., Kirilina, E., Haenelt, D., Pine, K., Trampel, R., Edwards, L., Weiskopf, N. (2020). Mapping short association fibers in the early cortical visual processing stream using in vivo diffusion tractography. Cerebral Cortex. doi:10.1093/cercor/bhaa049. Talks Haenelt, D., Weiskopf, N., Vaculciakova, V., Mueller, R., Nasr, S., Polimeni, J., Tootell, R., Huber, L., Sereno, M., Trampel, R. (2020). Mapping ocular dominance columns in humans using GE-EPI, SE-EPI and SS-SI-VASO at 7 T.
